sentences.text: In the ghetto, yes.
Well, as I said, it-- the Germans sort of were all around the ghetto.
Wanted us all out.
And we always prepared, sort of, it was a little trap door, where we went down, like in a little cellar.
There we were sitting.
I'll never forget it.
On one side, my late brother, on the other side, me, and mom in the middle.
And we were really shivering.
It was February in Europe.
It was very cold.
And all of a sudden, we heard the noise and the stamping, and the Poles said, here they are sitting, the Jews.
So we had to come out.
And we were gathered in the center of town.
And it went very, very quickly.
We were divided.
My mom was sent to one side, me to another side, and my brother to another side.
And what also partly saved my life, my mom went into her pocket and had some money and divided it between my brother and me.
And this amount, later on, partly saved my life.
Yes.
My mom saw to it that he'd come back.
She got him back, and she let him learn a profession, a trade.
Electrician at that time.
It was in the ghetto, yes.
That's right.
Yes.
My mother, unfortunately, went to Auschwitz.
My brother went into a-- it was a concentration camp.
[NON-ENGLISH].
In Silesia.
When I was in the camp, a girl my age was sent back.
And she told me she was sent back from Auschwitz because she was strong enough to work.
And they wanted her first to work and then to die.
And so she said she met my mother there.
That is-- yeah.
Yes.
Well, we-- we knew-- of course.
We knew that young people were sent to the labor camps.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] older people-- my mother was 46 when she was sent to Auschwitz.
Yes, because our-- [INAUDIBLE] was very close.
At nighttime, you saw from the burning of the-- of the stoves.
You saw the sky, red.
Not exactly the chimneys, but the reddish sky, from--
The glow.
The glow of the sky.
[INAUDIBLE].
That's right.
That's right.
Not-- not exactly, no.
I-- I never imagined it.
No.
I came in, and it was called [INAUDIBLE], to Sosnowitz, where, you know, from all parts abroad, they-- younger people there.
I was, at the time, 23.
Yes.
Yes, from 16 on to maybe and twenties.
No-- no older.
No, we were only women.
Because men went--
Yes.
I was only with women together.
I don't know whether at other times they brought men in.
Well, I was, for quite a while there, because I was German speaking.
So one German wanted that I make his correspondence and so on.
So I was a while there.
But then I saw it is completely empty already.
The girls were sent away to different camps.
And I practically was with a few girls only.
So I said, oh, no.
I don't want that.
And when they called out again for workers to-- to the camp I was in, I said, I'd better go to the camp.
And that was--
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
I didn't want to work there for a German because what would have happened out of me?
It would have been my end anyway.
When I would have served him well, then I thought, you know.
I found it better to be together with my people.
What I did.
To-- to go for work.
I came into-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH].
It is a working camp.
But sort of more than working camp, as if you would have done something bad--
And you were-- yes.
Like a punishing camp.
But [CHUCKLES] I didn't do anything bad.
It was just their description of it.
Neusalz [NON-ENGLISH].
That was also Silesia.
Silesia.
Yes.
It was a working camp.
We had-- we worked for [NON-ENGLISH], which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE].
What would that be--
A-- sort of a mill.
We did cottons.
Spinning yarn.
And it-- it was-- it was catastrophic, this thing, because we had-- it was so dusty from the spinning that you couldn't see the machine next to you.
So gray, dusty.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from time to time, we had a medical examination.
And supposed x-rays.
And whoever had something in the lungs, came straight-- was sent away to Auschwitz.
In [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '43.
It must have been on the 18th.
We were taken away from the ghetto.
Then I was--
February.
To Sosnowitz, and from there to the camp.
March.
March.
The beginning of March, because I wasn't too long.
No, no.
We were in trains.
Horrifying.
It had huge gates.
And over the gates [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in-- was written, arbeit macht frei.
Work makes free.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the whole impression was catastrophic.
With the little hats all around.
And the girls who were already there were thin like sticks.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was so shocked through losing my mother, I couldn't eat.
And practically, when I got two pieces of bread, which everybody got a day, they tore it out-- out of my hand.
You know, so hungry they were.
And I-- I just-- for a few days, I couldn't eat.
I was absolutely, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, I knew I lost my mother.
And that was enough for me to lose everything.
We-- no.
We were not shaven, but as soon as we came into the camp, we were bathed.
Showered.
And the good luck [INAUDIBLE] that they-- nursing sisters there, we had a Jewish nursing sister.
She was from my town.
And I knew her well.
So she came to me, and she said, when you have something of value, give it to me.
I'll keep it for you.
Because when you come out of the shower, you lose everything.
So what-- I had a little ring, which I gave her.
And I had the money which my mom gave me.
And she was quite right.
When we came out, in March, it would have been, with our-- they were like little nighties or cotton things.
Freezing like hell.
I'll never forget that.
And we were brought into the barracks.
So were you given prison clothing to wear?
With stripes?
They gave us, actually, clothes from other people.
They were not prison clothes.
They were civilian clothes, which I suppose they took from others and first dropped whatever was in it, and then distributed it to us.
That's right.
In the barracks, and from-- from there, actually, we went to every day to work.
And the work-- I worked in a spinnerei, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it was horrifying work.
Because the machines were very dangerous.
People-- girls lost their fingers.
Their hand came in.
And I was always one who was very careful with my body, not to injure my body.
So I remember I was standing at the machine, and the cotton, you know, the flax, actually, from the field, run and broke sometimes.
While the machine was running, we had to repair it.
Put it together.
Tie it up again.
Tie it up again.
And that was terrible dangerous.
And there was a German fellow who watched us or saw that I stopped the machine.
Then I put the thing-- then he said, I'll send you to Auschwitz.
And I said, but in one piece.
I couldn't help it, you know.
[CHUCKLES]
I wasn't punished for that.
But he watched me very sorely.
And you know, in life, all is sort of-- some are meant to live, and some are not meant to live.
This older fellow who supervised went away, and we got a German supervisor, a younger woman.
And she liked me very much.
And she never let me work.
She said, come, we'll clean the needles of the machine.
You'll come and talk with me.
So, in that respect, then, I had it not so hard, because I wasn't on those dangerous machines.
You know, when you're young, you have such a force that's in you that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] makes you hope you survive.
I actually never could imagine myself not to survive.
In the worst situation, I was lucky, because even in the ghetto, when [NON-ENGLISH], it was called.
That means when they surrounded the ghetto and picked people to send to the camps.
Even then I had luck.
I-- there was a policeman who said, come clean by the police station.
So I was already out of the danger.
And I had that several times, so it is-- I was meant to live, I only can say.
I-- look.
I never thought I wouldn't survive it.
I couldn't imagine myself how it will end.
But I always had the hope.
You know, I'll make it.
Especially when I came into the camp.
And I was-- I had the language, which was good.
I had the looks, because I was light blonde by nature.
So we sat alone.
I thought, I've got to help myself.
I've got to run away.
I always had the idea.
From the minute I came into the camp, I always worked towards it.
And after work, when we had to be called to rest, I volunteered to work by Germans in the garden.
And all I wanted, actually, was to find out the surroundings, to see where the station is.
Things like that.
In the town.
Slave labor.
Look, somebody works in the garden for nothing.
It's fantastic.
I volunteered for that.
I got to know the way to the station from the camp.
Yes, its surroundings.
Yes I had-- I took my decision when I weighed myself.
Like some material for the machines was weighed.
There I went, weighed myself.
And I had less than 100 pounds.
So I knew, when I don't do something drastically, I'd starve to death anyway.
So I thought when they catch me, well, it will be a sudden death.
And I organized myself with a girlfriend of mine, who actually was a Christian-born girl adopted by Jewish people.
What was her name?
Ushith Fiklitzer.
I teamed up with her.
And I waited for the chance until our barracks were painted.
And when my barrack was painted, we could sleep in other barracks to-- over the time, until it dried out.
That's right.
And at that time, I said, now is the time.
And I had to organized a wire cutter.
Little wire cutter, what we needed for the machines.
I just grabbed it.
And with this wire cutter, I cut a hole in the fence, behind-- actually, behind the [INAUDIBLE] station.
And with her, we had practically nothing with us.
A little bag.
With her, at night time, we slipped through this hole.
She's panicked a bit.
I go back, tell her, all right, I'll go forward.
And we-- we went then through a journey where German gardens.
We jumped the fence and banked through the garden into the street.
So my first-- our first way was to the station, away from where the [INAUDIBLE] was.
And I had good luck.
We had good luck in that at the same night, when we went to the station and boarded the train, this train came from a ways, with refugees from Hamburg.
Hamburg was completely bombarded.
So that was, for us, good luck, that nobody checked for papers, for it mostly would have been done.
And I listened to the conversations.
So straightforward, I said, I'm from Hamburg.
So you had a ready-made situation.
Yes, for me, actually, it happened to me.
Therefore I say I was meant to live.
Because otherwise, it would have been so much harder.
Ah, yes.
Yeah.
Well, when I-- before I went away, or ran away from the camp, I said, honey, I can buy some bread.
Could you give me my money?
And she gave it to the last cent.
No question asked.
So I had some money for tickets and eventually to buy something.
No.
That was too dangerous.
Because you know, to have a position as a nurse in the camp meant that you had to work with the Germans, otherwise you couldn't keep the position.
So I was much too clever for it to tell her my real intentions.
That's right.
No, no, no.
They were in the train, which we--
Yeah.
Which we--
Approximately, I think, six months.
I have the [INAUDIBLE] here and, it is a bit easier to-- six months.
Yeah.
That's right.
Yes.
I-- I think so.
I can't swear to it, the exact date.
I only know I was half a year in the camp.
That-- that I--
That's exactly right.
I went to Breslau, where this [INAUDIBLE], this manager of ours, where of course, I thought, he has so many properties and gardens and so on.
I-- I imagined myself, he will hide us.
Gustav Wagner.
To the ghetto, and even to the camp, when I was in the camp.
Twice he brought to the painter who painted the barracks, he brought food for me, and the painter gave it to me.
I always had some luck with people.
He got the biggest shock in his life.
And he said, Freulein Gerstl, [INAUDIBLE].
I can give you money.
But you have to disappear.
Try to come to Switzerland.
I'll give you enough money to go to Switzerland.
But please go away from here, and don't tell anyone that you were here.
So off we had to go.
And we-- Ushith had an aunt somewhere, and we went to the aunt, and the aunt got a shock.
And the quicker we had to go out, the better for her.
So she threw you out.
Straight.
Without giving her anything.
Like, I at least got some money to maybe help myself with it.
And then we actually, then she said, look, in, Chrzanow, where I was in the ghetto, there are some nice Polish people.
Let's go there.
And so she wanted.
And we took the train.
And the train took us over Auschwitz to Chrzanow.
And for the first time, I saw this special line which went to the camp.
And not to make.
And each station had, like, station police.
And I thought, my God, we must look a bit wild.
So I had [INAUDIBLE].
I went to the station police, and I started to-- to ask about when the next train comes.
I kept him in conversation, that he wouldn't get the idea, you know, to ask for paper, and so on and so on.
And I succeeded with that.
And then we-- we came to Chrzanow.
Nobody wanted to know us.
And she said, you know what?
In Katowice I have someone.
So we took from Chrzanow, again, we went back to Katowice.
And where she said, look I am the Aryan of us both.
I'll go and buy the tickets, and you sit down in the waiting room.
So I was sitting in the waiting room.
Sitting, sitting, and I thought, my God, that is a long time she doesn't come back.
And all stations have these [INAUDIBLE] polizei-- that means they check for papers.
Like in the restaurant I was sitting, they come in and ask everybody for papers.
And I thought that is my end.
So as they came once, I-- I went the other side out, so that I avoided them.
And I thought, I have to look at the counter.
At the ticket counter.
Where is she?
And there were two German civilians.
They looked like civilians, but they were these criminal polizei.
And I heard-- I was standing next to them, and I heard them say to each other, the one have-- we have only the other.
And I was exactly standing next to them.
So in a terrible hurry, I disappeared from the whole station.
So you realized that when they said we've got one, we don't have the other, they meant you, or she and you.
I knew what happened.
Yes.
They were looking already for me.
And I went to a little township, like a suburb, from Katowice, where parents lived from a girl I was in camp with.
And I-- not-- they were actually not parents.
They must have been relatives.
There must have been mixed marriages, because they could stay there.
So I suppose it must have been something mixed.
Anyway, I-- I said to them that I came here with a German [NON-ENGLISH], shopping for the camp.
It is like a-- not a police, but it is somebody who watches over you so that you don't run away, or so--
Like a supervisor.
And I said-- and she said to me, I have friends.
I can stay with friends.
So if you would be good enough to take me for the night, I'd prefer to stay privately with [INAUDIBLE].
She said, of course.
And they were very, very nice.
I stayed overnight there.
And then in my despair, I said, I haven't got any way out.
Even-- they got a shock when they saw me, now it's already maybe a week or 10 days later.
I go back to the Wagners, to Breslau.
